coredata.dc:title: Polyethyleneimine-functionalized carbon dots and their precursor co-immobilized on silica for hydrophilic interaction chromatography
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Tianpei
Zhang, Haijuan
Chen, Jia
Li, Zhan
Qiu, Hongdeng
coredata.dc:description: A new strategy based on synergistic effect of the carbon dots and their precursor was proposed to enhance the selectivity of hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography (HILIC). In this work, polyethyleneimine (PEI) and PEI-functionalized carbon dots (PEICDs) mix-grafted silica packing material was prepared to act as a novel HILIC stationary phase. Both inner and outer surface of the porous silica are decorated with the mixture of PEI and carbon dots. This stationary phase, namely Sil-PEI/CDs, demonstrate enhanced retention ability and selectivity toward polar analytes, with which 11 nucleosides and nucleobases and 9 ginsenosides can be nicely separated. The Sil-PEI/CDs (RSD 0.12% – 0.54%) exhibited even better stability than the traditional PEI modified stationary phase (RSD 0.39% – 0.87%) within 40 h of continuously elution. And excellent column efficiency was observed from Sil-PEI/CDs (∼65,000 plates/m for cytidine). The strategy of mixed stabilization revealed a new method to prepare good performanced carbon dots based chromatographic column.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyethyleneimine
Carbon dots
Hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography
Synergistic effect
Selectivity enhancement

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Chromatographic performance of Sil-PEI vs Sil-PEICDs vs Sil-PEI/CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of CDs (a), Sil-PEI (b), Sil-PEICDs (c), Sil-PEI/CDs (d); overlay of confocal fluorescence and bright-field images of Sil-PEICDs (e), Sil-PEI/CDs (f), (λex = 405 nm).

Separation of 11 nucleosides and nucleobases: thymine (1), uracil (2), thymidine (3), uridine (4), adenine (5), adenosine (6), hypoxanthine (7), cytosine (8), inosine (9), cytidine (10), guanine (11). Flow rate = 1.0 mL min−1, T = 35 °C, UV detection: 254 nm; mobile phase: 90% acetonitrile: 10% ammonium formate (20 mM, pH = 6.2).

Separation of 9 ginsenosides: Rh1 (1), Rg1 (2), Rg2 (3), Rd (4), Re (5), Rc (6), Rb3 (7), Rb2 (8), Rb1 (9); Flow rate = 1.0 mL min−1, T = 20 °C, mobile phase: 85% acetonitrile: 15% ammonium formate (10 mM, pH = 6.2).

(a) Ratio of the retention factor of cytosine to uracil with varying concentration of acetonitrile in mobile phase; (b) ratio of the retention factor of adenosine to adenine with varying concentration of acetonitrile in mobile phase. Conditions: flow rate = 1.0 mL min−1, T = 35 °C, UV detection: 254 nm; mobile phase: 8% ammonium formate aqueous solution (250 mM, pH = 6.2) and acetonitrile with varying contents of acetonitrile and water.

head.ce:title: Polyethyleneimine-functionalized carbon dots and their precursor co-immobilized on silica for hydrophilic interaction chromatography

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou, 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key Laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100039, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new strategy based on synergistic effect of the carbon dots and their precursor was proposed to enhance the selectivity of hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography (HILIC). In this work, polyethyleneimine (PEI) and PEI-functionalized carbon dots (PEICDs) mix-grafted silica packing material was prepared to act as a novel HILIC stationary phase. Both inner and outer surface of the porous silica are decorated with the mixture of PEI and carbon dots. This stationary phase, namely Sil-PEI/CDs, demonstrate enhanced retention ability and selectivity toward polar analytes, with which 11 nucleosides and nucleobases and 9 ginsenosides can be nicely separated. The Sil-PEI/CDs (RSD 0.12% – 0.54%) exhibited even better stability than the traditional PEI modified stationary phase (RSD 0.39% – 0.87%) within 40 h of continuously elution. And excellent column efficiency was observed from Sil-PEI/CDs (∼65,000 plates/m for cytidine). The strategy of mixed stabilization revealed a new method to prepare good performanced carbon dots based chromatographic column.

ce:section.ce:para: As the research focus of analytical chemistry shift from traditional research field to the complex biological sample analysis, the analysts encounter the separation of strong polar and hydrophilic analytes more frequently. However, as the most developed chromatographic technique, reverse phase liquid chromatography (RPLC) is not very effective for the analysis of water-soluble biological components, such as carbohydrates, amino acids, nucleobases and nucleosides [1]. On the other side, as an alternative to normal phase liquid chromatography (NPLC), the developing of a relative new chromatographic technique named hydrophilic interaction chromatography (HILIC) provide the analysts a better choice for the separation of the above mentioned samples [2], which featured by the hydrophilic stationary phase and the unique “partitioning” retention mechanism [3,4]. Besides, the organic-rich mobile phase makes it more compatible with mass spectrometry (MS) [5].

HILIC stationary phases can be classified into three kinds based on the type of bonding phases, including monomers [6,7], polymers [8–10] and nanomaterials [11]. And most of them are polar or hydrophilic species, such as saccharides, amide, carboxyl and zwitterionic molecules [12–14]. Matrix bonding with monomeric molecules is the most used strategy to prepare a HILIC stationary phase, which simply involved the immobilization of the readymade monomers. In order to get better hydrophilic selectivity, polymerization was introduced into the preparation of SP to get higher density of surface hydrophilic functional groups, which enabled better formation of water-rich layer [15].

Since nanomaterials became a hot point in the analytical science, the researchers have been intrigued by their unique property [16]. Their special surface functional groups and extremely high surface area make them promising material for sample pretreatment [17]. But when the nanomaterials were grafted on silica spheres as stationary phases, some nanomaterials (like graphene) may suffer the strong adsorption resulted from their large surface area and π-conjugated system. The strong adsorption could hinder rapid adsorption-desorption process of some specific analytes between mobile phase and stationary phase, which may cause low column efficiency and peak asymmetry [18]. Besides, nanomaterials like carbon nanotube, graphene and graphic carbon nanoparticle do not have hydrophilic nature, so their modification with hydrophilic species is necessary procedure when make them into HILIC stationary phase, which complicate the synthesis of stationary phase. In other words, a good HILIC nanomaterial stationary phase shall have moderate adsorbability, diversities of hydrophilic surface functional groups and easy to be processed, moreover, good shape to minimize the unnecessary eddy diffusion. As a good example, Armstrong et al. recently developed a novel aluminosilicate ceramic solid packing material which have hydrophilic nature, porous spherical morphology, so the column packed with this material shows excellent peak shapes and hydrophilic selectivity [19].

Our group comes up with the idea to used carbon dots (CDs) as HILIC stationary phase, which not only have sphere-like morphology, but also have many hydrophilic types. As a big advantage compared with traditional carbon nanomaterials, CDs can be synthesized from molecular precursors with facile method, and the as synthesized CDs can incorporate hydrophilic surface functional groups, like polyethylene glycol (PEG), ethylenediamine, polyethyleneimine (PEI) etc. [20]. Besides, CDs also avoid the terrible aggregation which other carbon nanomaterials usually encounter.

In our group’s former work, a carbon dot-decorated silica stationary phase was prepared in deep eutectic solvents, which the carbon dot was obtained via a solid-phase synthesis approach using tryptophan and aconitic acid as ingredients [21]. This stationary phase exhibited good potential to separate nucleosides and nucleobases, despite tailing peaks were obtained which mainly due to the carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs. In order to improve the chromatographic performance, N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl-methyldimethoxysilane (AEAPMS) was used to prepare silanized carbon dots, which can be grafted on to silica sphere directly. The obtained packing materials possess short chain alkylamines, which showed enhanced selectivity towards middle polar and hydrophilic analytes than the AEAPMS modified silica stationary phase [22].

In this work, PEI was selected as a hydrophilic precursor to produce a polyamine-functionalized carbon dots (PEICDs), since PEI contain much more amine groups. On the basis of sole immobilization of the carbon dots, we tried for the first time to prepare the stationary phase by co-immobilization of carbon dots and their precursor on silica, because co-immobilization is believed to be an effective method to improve the performance of stationary phases [23,24]. So, PEI and PEICDs co-immobilized silica stationary phase (Sil-PEI/CDs) were prepared (Fig. 1 ). By comparison, PEI-modified silica (Sil-PEI) and PEICDs-grafted silica (Sil-PEICDs) stationary phases was also prepared. The authors believe, by study the chromatographic performance differences of these stationary phases which have diverse contents of CDs and PEI, pluses with characterization of the CDs’ surface property, the synergistic effect of CDs and their precursor can be revealed.

All materials and reagents were used directly without purification. Spherical porous silica (diameter: 5 μm, pore size: 100 Å, surface area: 306 m2 g−1) were obtained from Fuji Silysia Chemical Ltd. (Aichi, Japan,). Polyethyleneimine (M = 1800, 99%) 3-glycidoxypropyltrimethoxysilane (GPTMS) (97%) and anhydrous citric acid (CA) (99%) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Nucleosides and nucleobases, including thymine, thymidine, uracil, uridine, adenosine, adenine, hypoxanthine, inosine, cytosine, cytidine and guanine were all supplied by Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Ginsenosides (Rb1, Rb2, Rb3, Rc, Rd, Re, Rh1, Rg1, Rg2) were purchased from Chengdu Push Bio-technology CO., TLD (Chengdu, China). Flavonoid glycosides (hesperidin, salicin, naringin, amygdalin) were bought from Chengdu MUST Bio-technology CO., TLD (Chengdu, China). Other chemicals and solvents were purchased from Energy Chemical (Shanghai, China).

For comparison, four commercial columns were used, including XBridge HILIC column, XBridge BEH Amide column (both purchased from Waters), PRONTOSIL 120-5-DIOL column (purchase from ProntoSIL) and GS-120-5-APS column (supported by Global Chromatography). The size of all these columns are 4.6 mm i.d. × 150 mm length and the packing materials are 5 μm particles. The tests of nucleosides and nucleobases were carried out with an Agilent 1260 Infinity Series modular system with quaternary pumps and diode array detector (Agilent Technologies, USA), 254 nm wavelength was chosen for the UV/vis detector. The test of ginsenosides and saccharides were processed on Agilent 1260 Infinity Series modular system with quaternary pumps (Agilent Technologies, USA), an Alltech 3300 evaporative light scattering detector (Grace, USA) with GCK3302 air generator (BCHP Analytical Technology Institute, China). The ELSD was set as follows: gas flow, 1.5 L min−1; evaporative temperature, 65 °C; photomultiplier, 1; gas pressure, 0.50 Mpa. The FTIR spectra were collected from IFS 120HR Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Elemental analysis results were determined by Vario EL III elemental analyzer (Hanau, Germany). Transmission electron microscopies (TEM) imaging were acquired from Tecnai G2 TF20 transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). Laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM) imaging were attained from Laser Scanning Confocal Microscope FV1200 (Olympus, Japan). N2 adsorption surface areas were measured by BET technique on a Micrometritics ASPS 2010 analyzer (USA).

PEICDs were prepared by an easy one-step pyrolysis of CA in the aqueous solution of PEI according to a referencing method [25]. Briefly, 0.5 g of PEI and 1.0 g anhydrous citric acid were dissolved in 10 mL water. Then the solvent was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. The resulted mixture was purified by dialyzing against ultrapure water with a cellulose ester membrane bag (Mw = 3500) to remove unreacted PEI. Then the purified carbon dots suspension was vacuum distilled and lyophilized to get the dried PEICD.

Spherical porous silica (3 g) was dispersed ultrasonically into 25 mL of toluene, followed by adding 6 mmol of 3-glycidoxypropyltrimethoxysilane, and then the suspension was refluxed for 24 h in nitrogen atmosphere. The resulted silanized silica (Sil-GPTMS) was centrifuged and washed successively with anhydrous toluene and ethanol.

Because the PEICDs suffered poor solubility in traditional organic solvent, and the stabilization of PEICDs onto the abovementioned silanized silica need anhydrous atmosphere, so deep eutectic solvent (DES) were chosen as the reaction solvent of the following step, which not only showed good solubility for PEICDs, but also can maintain fine suspension of silica for long time. The DES was prepared according to a former published work. Briefly, choline chloride (ChCl) and glycol were mixed together with a molar ratio of 1:3 and magnetic stirred at 80 °C until a homogeneous liquid was formed [26].

For the preparation of Sil-PEICDs, 0.8 g of PEICDs and the former produced silanized silica were added into 20 mL DES, and the mixture was ultrasonicated to form a homogeneous suspension. Then the suspension was mechanical stirring at 65 °C for 24 h, after that, the resulted material was washed successively with water, ethanol and methanol, then dried at 60 °C in vacuum oven.

For the preparation of Sil-PEI/CDs, 0.8 g of PEICDs and 0.4 g of PEI and the former produced silanized silica were added into 20 mL DES, and the following procedure is identical to the preparation of Sil-PEICDs.
As a comparative experiment, the Sil-PEI were prepared by adding 1.2 g of PEI and former produced silanized silica into 20 mL acetonitrile, and the following procedure is the same as above [27].

In order to characterize the reproducibility of the preparation of the stationary phase coatings, three batches of Sil-PEI/CDs were prepared, and the chromatographic parameters can be well reproduced (see Fig. S1 for reproducibility results).

Sil-PEI, Sil-PEICDs and Sil-PEI/CDs stationary phases were dispersed into ultrapure water to make slurry, then the dispersions were packed into stainless steel columns (150 mm × 4.6 mm i.d.) using methanol as propulsion solvent, separately.

Multiple methods were used to prove the successful stabilization of carbon dots or PEI onto the silica spheres. The transmission electron microscopy of PEICDs, Sil-PEI, Sil-PEICDs and Sil-PEI/CDs appear in Fig. 2 , in which the average particle size of CDs can be obtained by counting the size of 400 carbon dots particles in the TEM image (Fig. 2a). The average particle size of PEICDs is around 2.5 nm (Fig. S2). Instead of the smooth spherical surface of Sil-PEI (Fig. 2b), the surface of Sil-PEICDs and Sil-PEI/CDs exhibited rough appearance (Fig. 2c and d) which indicated a thin layer of PEICDs was modified on the silica sphere. Besides, the fluorescent property of PEICDs allows the coverage of carbon dots on silica to be characterized by laser scanning confocal microscope. According to the overlay confocal fluorescence and bright-field images of Sil-PEICDs and Sil-PEI/CDs (Fig. 2e and f), each of the silica sphere were covered with homogeneous fluorescence, which means the bonding reaction of PEICDs with silica was effectively proceed. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to prove the individual or mixed stabilization of PEI and PEICDs on the silica (Fig. S3). PEICD, Sil-PEI, Sil-PEICDs and Sil-PEI/CDs share a same absorption peak of 1651 cm−1 (corresponding to the bending vibration of NH), which means the PEICD inherited the polyamine structure of PEI and the Sil-PEI was successfully prepared. Furthermore, a new absorption peak showed at 1574 cm-1 was associated with the formation of amide linkage (C = ONR), which indicates the surface passivation of PEI on carbon dots, so this peak can be treated as a typical signal of PEICDs. And this means PEI is covalently bonded with the carbon dot, since amino groups of PEI can react with carboxyl groups on the carbon dot through amidation. Thus, when the Sil-PEICDs and Sil-PEI/CDs both exhibited this absorption, it is reasonable to believe PEICDs has been grafted onto the above packing materials. One thing worth to be mentioned is that the absorption intensity ratio of 1651 cm−1 to 1574 cm-1 in the spectrum of Sil-PEI/CDs is much higher than that in the spectrum of Sil-PEICDs, which confirmed the PEI and PEICDs were mixed stabilized on the Sil-PEI/CDs.

Elemental analysis results of PEICDs, Sil-GPTMS, Sil-PEI, Sil-PEICDs and Sil-PEI/CDs were shown in Table S1. Because the mass ratio of C to N (mC/mN) of PEICDs is different from that of PEI, calculate from which, the bonding ratio of PEI to PEICDs is nPEI/nPEICD = 1.22 (see the Supporting Information for calculations).

The BET surface area measurements were performed. As listed in Table S2, the pore volumes of Sil-PEI, Sil-PEICDs and Sil-PEI/CDs particles were tested to be 0.83, 0.37 and 0.46 cm3 g−1, respectively. The pore size distribution estimated by the density functional theory (BJH) showed a maximum at 11.02, 4.87 and 5.07 nm for Sil-PEI, Sil-PEICDs and Sil-PEI/CDs particles, respectively. The BET analysis results show that the BET surface areas and pore sizes and pore volume of Sil-PEICDs were all much lower than those of Sil-PEI, which indicate that PEICDs were not just grafted on the surface of silica spheres, but also imbedded into the pores of the silica spheres. Besides, the pore sizes and pore volume of Sil-PEI/CDs showed a slightly increase compared with Sil-PEICDs, which means the existence of PEI occupied some bonding sites and inevitably lower the amount of stabilized PEICDs on the pore surface. Benefit from this, some nanopores which were blocked by carbon dots got reopen, so the BET surface area of Sil-PEI/CDs received a remarkably increase than Sil-PEICDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chromatographic performance of Sil-PEI vs Sil-PEICDs vs Sil-PEI/CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In our former work, we have proved that the carbon dots stationary phase has better chromatographic performance than its precursor modified stationary phase. Based on this discovery, we are expecting a cooperativity of carbon dot and its precursor could provide better chromatographic selectivity, so Sil-PEI/CDs was also added into the comparison. The separation of 11 nucleosides and nucleobases on the three columns were all carried out under the same elution condition. As shown in Fig. 3 , even Sil-PEI shows the strongest retention ability, it cannot get the best separation selectivity toward these analytes. Sil-PEI was able to separate 9 of all the 11 analytes, in which thymine and thymidine, cytidine and guanine can only be partly separated. Unlike Sil-PEI, Sil-PEICDs exhibit the worst retention ability and separation selectivity, with which the separation of adenine, adenosine hypoxanthine and cytosine are inefficient. Surprisingly, all the 11 nucleosides and nucleobases can be separated on Sil-PEI/CDs, even the operation time is only half of that of the Sil-PEI. This result implies that the coexistence of PEI and PEICD enhanced the selectivity of the stationary phase, which can be regarded as a proof for the cooperativity of carbon dot and its precursor.

Furthermore, 9 ginsenosides were also chosen as probes to check the cooperativity of PEI and PEICDs by comparing the separation performance of Sil-PEI, Sil-PEICDs and Sil-PEI/CDs. Fig. 4 demonstrate that under the identical elution conditions, Sil-PEI shows much weaker retention ability for ginsenosides than that of Sil-PEICDs and Sil-PEI/CDs. Sil-PEICDs and Sil-PEI/CDs exhibit similar selectivity, but Sil-PEI/CDs own even stronger retention ability which allows it to have better resolutions toward the analytes. This result also proves the existence of cooperativity, which are consistent with the result of nucleosides and nucleobases. Besides, in order to further prove the existence of cooperativity, 4 flavonoid glycosides were also selected as probes. As shown in Fig. S4, hesperidin, naringin and amygdalin cannot be separated on Sil-PEICDs, and salicin showed the weakest retention. Since hesperidin, naringin and amygdalin all contain a disaccharide residue while salicin contains a monosaccharide residue, it is reasonable to believe the retention of flavonoid glycosides on Sil-PEICDs relay on the hydrophilic interactions between sugar residues and the stationary phase. On the other hand, hesperidin and naringin showed weaker retention on Sil-PEI compared to Sil-PEICDs, which may relate to the interactions between the aglycones and PEI. However, Sil-PEI/CDs exhibited the best selectivity and retention ability towards the flavonoid glycosides compared with the other two columns, in which the retention of salicin, naringin and amygdalin were remarkably enhanced. So, the separation results toward the flavonoid glycosides are also positive evidences for the existence of cooperativity between PEI and PEICDs.

Because most known ginsenosides are classified as members of the dammarane family, which consists of aglycone moieties (2 or 3 hydroxyl groups) and their sugar residues [28]. The structural differences of the ginsenosides are due to the numeric and structural difference of the monosaccharide units of the sugar residues and the positional difference of sugar residues on the aglycone (see Fig. S5). So, when separate ginsenosides under HILIC mode, the separation of the analytes mainly relies on the above-mentioned differences which allowing different extent of retention ability of the analytes on the stationary phase. Given the ginsenosides are unionizable molecules, the portioning mechanism and hydrogen bonding affinity instead of the electrostatic interaction would play the vital roles during the separation process.

The comparison of the separation performance toward 11 nucleosides and nucleobases with four commercial columns and Sil-PEI/CDs was also performed (see Fig. S6). Through the comparison results, we can see Sil-PEI/CDs exhibit the strongest retention ability under the same operation conditions, followed by the amino and amide functionalized columns, the diol functionalized column and the BEH HILIC column shows the weakest retention. It seems like low buffer concentration is more compatible with amino containing stationary phase, instead of diol functionalized or unmodified packing materials.

According to the model proposed by Gritti et al. [29], which based on the premise that the interface between the solid silica surface and the liquid mobile phase is formed by a water rich layer with a rigid and diffuse part, the partitioning correlated to the diffuse water layer while the adsorption needs the analytes retention in rigid water layer. Unlike the reversed phase stationary phase which can be designed with single retention mechanism of hydrophobic interaction, it requires high concentration of salt (at least 20 mM of overall concentration) in the mobile phase to titrate the HILIC stationary phase and complete the electrical double layer on the stationary phase surface, which allow a HILIC stationary phase involves hydrophilic partitioning mechanism alone [29–31]. Since the HILIC mode separation usually rely on both partitioning and adsorption mechanisms, which may enable better selectivity [32], it would be inappropriate to described the retention behavior of HILIC with individual partitioning or adsorption model [1]. So, a more appropriate quantitative retention model was established to fit the retention behavior of HILIC column [33]:
(1)
l og
k = a +
b log
φ + c φ
in which, k is the retention factor, a, b, and c are constants, and φ is the volume fraction water in the eluent. This equation seems like the combination of partitioning model equation [34] and adsorption model equation [35] which showed good accuracy in HILIC mode [36]. Fig. S7 and Table S3 demonstrate the fitting results for 10 nucleosides and nucleobases at five different mobile phase compositions (φ 0.08-0.24) on Sil-PEI (r2 0.9978-0.9999), Sil-PEICDs (r2 0.9948-0.9992) and Sil-PEI/CDs (r2 0.9978-0.9999) columns [37].

Since the penetration of the analytes into the rigid water layer can be hindered by high salt concentration, the extent of adsorption should be reduced by the increasing salt concentration. As shown in Fig. S8a, the retention ability of Sil-PEI toward the nucleosides and nucleobases decreases when increasing buffer concentration. This is probably because the retention of nucleosides and nucleobases on Sil-PEI is dominated by adsorption instead of partitioning. In contrast, the retention of nucleosides and nucleobases on Sil-PEICDs favor the partitioning, which get enhanced with higher buffer concentration (Fig. S8b). This is probably due to that the water rich layer on the stationary phase would become thicker, which is believed to promote the partitioning, as the buffer concentration increases [38]. As for Sil-PEI/CDs (Fig. S8c), most of the analytes are compatible with the partitioning mechanism, which exhibit enhanced retention at higher buffer concentration. However, hypoxanthine, inosine and adenine exhibit slightly U-shaped retention curve as the buffer concentration increases, which means both partitioning and adsorption mechanism may play vital roles in the retention mechanisms of these three analytes.

One curious fact is that, even the grafted amount of PEI on Sil-PEI/CDs is higher than that of PEICDs (mass ratio), the retention trends of the analytes (except hypoxanthine, inosine and adenine) on Sil-PEI/CDs toward the buffer concentration changing shows more similarity to that of Sil-PEICDs, instead of acting as the neutralization of Sil-PEI and Sil-PEICDs. Additionally, Sil-PEI/CDs exhibit stronger retention ability than Sil-PEICDs in a wide range of water contents in mobile phase (see Fig. S7). These facts prove that the existence of synergistic promoting effect of PEI and PEICDs when they were co-immobilized on silica as stationary phase.

When the pH of the mobile phase changed from neutral to acidic, nucleosides and nucleobases analytes as well as all three amino containing stationary phases would carry an increasing degree of positive charge, which may lead to earlier elution of the positive charged analytes due to electrostatic repulsion. So, the influence of pH on the retention ability of nucleosides and nucleobases was studied (as shown in Fig S9). The results show that all the analytes exhibited different extent of retention time decrease, which are consistent with the presumption above. Among which, the retention ability of Sil-PEI is most sensitive to the change of mobile phase pH, followed by Sil-PEI/CDs and Sil-PEICDs. According to the nitrogen contents of the stationary phases gotten from elemental analysis results (Table S1), this phenomenon is probably because the amount of electrostatic interaction sites (amino group) on Sil-PEI is more than that of Sil-PEI/CDs, while the Sil-PEICDs own the least amount of electrostatic interaction sites.

In summary, synergistic promoting effect of PEI and PEICDs was found in the retention trends of analytes on Sil-PEI/CDs toward the changes of water content in mobile phase and buffer concentration, this parameter significantly influences the hydrophilic interactions between stationary phase and analytes. However, no synergistic promoting effect was found in the retention trends of analytes on Sil-PEI/CDs toward the pH changing, which only behave as the neutralization of Sil-PEI and Sil-PEICDs. Since the hydrophilic interactions are independent of electrostatic interaction [30], we can deduce that the synergistic promoting effect is probably related to hydrophilic interactions, which is going to be studied in the next section.

Irgum and co-workers established a method to probing the interactions mode in HILIC by using the relative retention of a series of substance pairs [39,40]. kcytosine/kuracil can be used as a generic measure of the “hydrophilic” character of HILIC columns, the higher kcytosine/kuracil indicate better hydrophilicity [39,41]. Fig. 5 a demonstrates the trends of kcytosine/kuracil at the water contents between 0.08-0.24 with all three columns. The results show that all the three columns exhibit increasing hydrophilicity when water content in mobile phase decrease. The hydrophilicity of Sil-PEI seems to be more sensitive to the change of eluent composition than Sil-PEICDs and Sil-PEI/CDs. Besides, Sil-PEI demonstrates the strongest hydrophilicity under the whole testing range. However, due to the combination of PEI and PEICDs, Sil-PEI/CDs shows stronger hydrophilicity than Sil-PEICDs, but still not as strong as Sil-PEI.

kadenosine/kadenine is an index for multipoint hydrogen bonding [39]. Interesting phenomenon was observed from the comparison, the Sil-PEI/CDs showed the strongest multipoint hydrogen bonding interaction under all operating conditions when overall salt concentration was set as 20 mM (see Fig. 5b). Worth to be noticed, the elution of adenosine and adenine on these three tested columns all suffered elution order reverse as the water concentration of the mobile phase decrease, the reverse point is around 23% of water content for Sil-PEI/CDs, 17% of water content for Sil-PEICDs and 14% of water content for Sil-PEI. It is anomalous that adenosine which own a sugar residue would retention worse than its nucleobase in HILIC. A similar phenomenon was also observed by Alpert [31], in which cytosine sulfate was better retained than cytidine sulfate. In his paper, this phenomenon was explained by the strong orientation of hydrogen bonding between analytes and stationary phase. In details, if the retention of cytosine sulfate involves hydrogen bonding through position 1 of the cytosine ring, the introduction of ribose residue at this position would probably disrupt the oriented hydrogen bonding, which causes the retention decrease of cytidine, even the ribose residue may increase the hydrophilicity of the molecule. If use this theory to explain the anomalous elution order of adenosine and adenine, it would be the oriented hydrogen bonding through position 1 of the adenine plays the vital role on the stronger retention of adenine. One important thing needed to be clarified is that the above-mentioned oriented hydrogen bonding differs from the multipoint hydrogen bonding probed by kadenosine/kadenine according to the study of Dinh et al. [39]. They also claimed that increasing water content in the mobile phase would reduce the multipoint hydrogen bonding interactions, which is consist with the trends in Fig. 5b of the paper.

The enhanced multipoint hydrogen bonding exhibited on Sil-PEI/CDs can also explain why Sil-PEI/CDs demonstrate the highest retention ability towards the ginsenosides since the hydrophilicity of Sil-PEI are even higher than that of Sil-PEI/CDs. In fact, the synergistic effect of the PEICD and PEI is probably realized by enhancing multipoint hydrogen bonding interaction between stationary phase and analytes.

Because the PEICDs were self-assembled by pyrolysis, so the longevity of such stationary phase would be a critical factor to be concern. In order to check the stabilities of the stationary phases, the retention of five compounds including uracil, uridine, cytosine, cytidine, 4-N-butylbenzoic acid were studied for 40 h of continuous elution [42,43]. To ensure the rigorousness of the comparison, the elution time of each injection were modulated to be around 10 min for all these three columns. The RSD values (n = 9) of the retention times of the analytes were calculated within 0.39% – 0.87%, 0.16% – 0.45% and 0.12% – 0.54% for Sil-PEI, Sil-PEICDs and Sil-PEI/CDs, respectively (see Fig. S10). These results indicate that, instead of decreasing the stability, the carbon dots modified stationary phase exhibit even slightly better robustness than the bonded stationary phase of their precursor. Furthermore, the Sil-PEICDs and Sil-PEI/CDs enabled better column efficiencies than Sil-PEI (Fig. S11), which stay around 65,000 plates/m for cytidine compared to 40,000 plates/m of Sil-PEI.

PEI passivated carbon dots were stabilized on silica individually and simultaneously with PEI. Synergistic effect was found within the co-immobilized stationary phase, Sil-PEI/CDs, which exhibited enhanced selectivity, multipoint hydrogen bonding interaction and retention ability toward polar analytes. Sil-PEI/CDs also showed better column efficiency compared to that of Sil-PEI.

The positive results get from the strategy of mixed stationary phase preparation lift the performance of carbon dots modified stationary phase to a higher level. This strategy can even apply to the preparation of other nanomaterial-based stationary phases in the future. Moreover, this discovery allowed the good performanced carbon dots stationary phase to be prepared with an easy way, in which strict purification of CDs from their passivate agents is not needed. In fact, if the CDs were made by solventless reaction, the resulted mixture can be directly used to modify the chromatographic matrix.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge acknowledge the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21822407, 21675164, 21611140105).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chroma.2019.03.026.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
